title: Five-Minute Gut Check
subtitle: A short meditation for asking the body what it already knows
excerpt: A five-minute meditation for the body's reading of a question the head is already overthinking. The body has a faster answer than the head, and a shorter one. The practice teaches you to listen for it.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The body-as-second-brain frame draws on the work of Antonio Damasio on somatic markers, and on the wider literature on the enteric nervous system.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the head is going in circles on a question and the answer is not getting any clearer. Also useful before a meeting or before a difficult conversation.

content.text: A five-minute meditation that asks the body for its reading of a question. The head has been turning the question for an hour. The body usually has a shorter answer and a clearer one.
New to meditation as a practice? Read
Sit somewhere quiet. Put both feet on the floor. Take three slow breaths.
Bring the question to mind in one short sentence. Not the long version. The short one. "Should I take the job?" "Should I say yes to the trip?" "Should I send the message?"
Say the question to yourself, with one possible answer attached. "Should I take the job? Yes."

Notice what the body does for the next three breaths. Does the chest open or close? Does the belly settle or grip? Do the shoulders drop or pull up? Do not pick what you should be feeling. Notice what is there.
Now say the question with the other answer. "Should I take the job? No."
Notice the body again for three breaths. Which version does the body open into? Which version does it pull away from?
Open the eyes if they were closed. The body has had a vote. The head may still want to argue with it. That is alright. Most of the time, the body got there first and was right.
Carry the reading into the next thing. If the question is not ready to be answered today, the body has still given you data for when it is.
Original to homemade.education. The body-as-second-brain frame draws on the work of Antonio Damasio on somatic markers. It also draws on the wider writing about the gut as a sensing system.
